sentences.text: Yeah.
Right, correct.
Because nobody else survived.
If they would have survived, I would have known.
Yes.

But they were-- the Ukrainians came in the day that they got everybody together, and they were-- they were sleeping in an attic, and just got them all and brought them back to the ghetto together with all the other people, and that's when they were killed.
There was nobody else left.
That's right.
Yeah, they were brought back.
Yes.
That's why I survived because I was hiding.
My father made a hiding place under a twin bed, as big as a twin bed in-- in the house.
And we had some supplies, didn't last that long.
And there-- there were eight of us in that hiding place.
There was my aunt, her five-year-old granddaughter, my two cousins, my father and mother, and my sister and I.
That's right.
We were in that hiding place.
There was a lot of looting upstairs.
Every once in a while, I would hear some screaming outside.
The Ukrainian would catch somebody.

They would-- I don't know exactly where they took them, but I found out later there's a special-- in a special place near there-- near the woods that they made one big hole, grave, and they put everybody who they found there, they bring them there and kill them, and bury them right there.
And it was very difficult in that hiding place.
It's much easier to live without food than without water.
So one day, the five-year-old little girl said if you don't give me a drink of water, I'm going to scream.
So having no choice, we gave her urine to drink.
She never asked for another drink.
And one day, my father went upstairs to the attic and he brought back some matches.
I don't know why he was hiding them, what he did with them.
He came down.
He lit a match.
And when I looked around and saw the people of my family-- I wish I could paint so I could paint that kind of picture, because I always see it in front of me.
And then I really feel that my father lost his mind in-- mind in this hiding place, because we did-- we were there three weeks, and we decided we have to get out.
It's impossible to stay there.
But my father said, there are Nazis sitting up in the room.
We have to knock three times and ask in German, may we come up?
Doesn't make sense.
But to humor him, we did just that.
We decided every night, two people would go out so the rest could stretch.
And since my sister was a country teacher about 14 kilometers from the city, and whenever the farmers would come to the city, they would eat and sleep in her house.
So we thought if we could ever get to that village, they'll help us And somehow we got out of the ghetto.
The ghetto was covered with tallit and tefillin.
Those are religious articles.
Because that's one thing that Ukrainians had no use for.
And we were caught six-- six kilometers from the ghetto by Ukrainian standing guard.
My sister and I.
It was at night, because we never went daytime.
We were afraid of daylight, terrible.
We were quite a sight to see.
So he was beating us and taking us in.
And we had a few pieces of jewelry, my father's pocket watch.
We begged him to let us go.
He wouldn't hear of it, just taking us in.
And all of a sudden he said, I don't want anything from you.
Just leave the village.
And we started to run because it was almost daylight, and we were afraid of daylight.
And somehow we got into a farm.
We stayed there during the day, and we started walking at night to the village where my sister used to teach.
When we first got there, to people we knew, we asked them if they could keep us for the winter.
We were dreamers.
We learned quickly that you ask for one day at a time.
If there were no visiting neighbors and no barking dogs, we had a chance to warm up.
It got worse and worse.
People we knew threatened us and chased us.
There were partisans around, Russian partisans that would welcome us, and Ukrainian partisans that would kill us.
The languages are very similar and we spoke both.
And we wanted to join the Russian partisans.
So one night, we heard them.
We got frightened.
We couldn't make out who was who because of the language.
We hid until they passed by.
And we were so wet and cold and hungry, nobody would let us in anymore.
So we decided to go to the forest.
Maybe it'll be warmer there.
As we approached the forest, I saw a little white house with smoke coming out of a chimney.
What a beautiful sight it was, and how we envied the people there.
As we approached the house, we heard singing.
And my sister said, we can't go in there.
They're drinking.
So we started to run back in snow to our waist, about half a kilometer.
The winters are terrible in the Ukraine.
I sat down and refused to go any further because a farmer once told me, to fall asleep on a cold winter night is an easy death.
And my sister knew what I was thinking.
So she said to me, it's not so easy to die.
Get up and let's keep on walking.
I said no.
You go where and do as you please.
I'm going back to back to that little white house.
Something was pulling me there.
She followed me reluctantly.
When we approached that little white house-- I call it my miracle house-- there was no more singing, and this lovely woman came to the door, and she said please, welcome, come in.
Couldn't figure it out.
Here people chasing, threaten us, and this woman is inviting us in.
But I didn't care anymore.
She gave us hot food.
She washed and boiled our clothes because we were full of lice.
She gave us a bed to sleep in.
That was a real luxury.
They were very poor people.
And they-- they had three children, a husband and wife, and the two of us were sleeping in one room.
And they have a kitchen.
Then her husband came home, and he said-- rather, she said to him, I took in two young girls for the night.
He said, you did the right thing.
We couldn't figure it out.
But when the couple went to bed, they knelt and they prayed in their own words, dear God, save the Israelite remnants.
That's the first time I ever heard anybody refer to us as Israelites.
The next day, since there were no telephones-- so that-- that-- he was a Ukrainian Pentecostal preacher.
He had a deep voice, and that's why I was so scared.
I never heard of-- of Protestants.
I never knew they existed.

And since there were no telephones, he couldn't call anybody, he just said to us in the morning, you don't look Jewish, and between our brothers and sisters, we'll find a place for you, and you won't have to hide.
And he left.
He went to talk to a Polish Baptist, and in the evening that Polish Baptist came to the house and took me to his farm, and my sister stayed there for a while.
When I came to the farm, I found two Jewish boys hidden under the oven.
And the ovens we have there are not like here.
We have bakery ovens that you can hide under them.
And there was another Jewish boy taking care of the horses and cow.
And I joined the staff.
I call it as a mate.
They were very, very poor people.
They had one child, one son.
But they were very kind in sharing.
A week later, my sister joined me, but he couldn't keep her there.
There was too many of us.
So he said to her, he'll give her a few leads so she can go and see if she can get a job.
And she really didn't look Jewish at all.
I have very curly hair.
She had straight hair and a button nose that I envied.
She got a job on a Czech farm, on a Czech colony.
She told them that the reason she doesn't know how to work on a farm or to milk a cow was because she used to be a maid in a Jewish house.
And one day, a policeman, a friend of theirs came to the house and recognized her.
The reason he didn't make trouble for the families because they were his friends.
So he said, I'm leaving, but when I come back here, I don't want to see her here again.
So she left.
Of course, she denied being Jewish, but she left and she came to me, and my farmer couldn't keep her there.
So he gave her a few leads and told her to go.
And I didn't know what to do with myself because I felt very guilty.
So I decided to go with her.
But she made it easy for me.
She said, two-- nobody will hire two people.
You stay here and I'll go to the people he is telling me to go.
After that, I did not see my sister until the war was over.
I-- the-- the Ukrainian Banderovcis started to kill the Poles, for whom they had no love either.
Because you see the Germans promised them an independent Ukraine if they help them with the Jewish problem.
But after there were no more Jews, they said to them, there's not gonna be an independent Ukraine.
And that's when they formed a partisan group called Banderovcis, after their leader, Bandera, and killing a hiding Jew whenever they could find one.
So the people I was staying with had to leave the village because they were Poles.
And that Pentecostal farmer used to have, call it a meeting house where he lived.
People from different-- different denominations would come to the house to pray.
They were Protestants, Pentecostals, Seven Day Adventists, Evangelists, all kinds.
I never even knew they existed.
And they used to speak in tongues.
And that's all they have, the two rooms.
You could find one family in a village.
When my Polish family left, I followed them a few days later to the city.
Not my city, but a different city.
They couldn't keep me there.
So one day on a Sunday, they took me to the city of Rowne.
That's a bigger city.
And I went with them on a train.
I felt comfortable going with them on the train.
And there was Juden free already, no more Jews.
And when we came to the city, they took me to the-- a meeting house, they call it, a Protestant meeting house.
And as we walked in, we parted company pretending not to know each other.
After the sermon, I approached the reverend and I told them I was a Polish orphan girl.
My parents were killed by the Ukrainian Banderovcis.
And I am looking for work.
Could he find me a job?
He called over a director of a candy factory, and he asked him if he could help me.
He said sure, come tomorrow to the factory.
Well, tomorrow is a long time from today.
Where am I gonna sleep?
So having no choice, I said to the director, I have no place to stay.
So he called over this man and he said to him, this sister's gonna sleep in your house tonight.
Tomorrow when you come to work, bring her with you.
I went home with him, and the next day his wife made sandwiches for us, and we went to the factory.
It was a candy factory.
The city is full of Germans, and I was terribly frightened.
When I approached the director and I reminded him of his promise he made in church, he said I'm very sorry.
You can see how busy I am now.
Come back in two weeks.
Well, two weeks is a lifetime.
Having no choice, I said to him, may I speak to you in private?
He took me into his office, and I said, you know, I'm Jewish.
His eyes popped out.
He said, you are an Israelite.
That's the second time I heard that word, Israelite.
If so, I have to help you.
You've got to come from the Ukraine to appreciate this, because as a Polish orphan girl, he wasn't helping me.
As an Israelite, he was.
So I stayed with that family.
I went-- every day I went to to work, to the factory, until one day I came home and there was a woman visiting, and she took one look at me, and she said you are a [?
Haika ?], meaning the name for Jewish girl.
I said, don't be silly.
I walked out of the house, and I never came back.
I went to the director of the candy factory.
I told him what happened.
He said to me, I've got to find the place for you on a farm with a Protestant family, because the city is no place for you.
In the meantime, sleep in the factory.
And that's what I did.
And then he tried to make me a passport.
That was really something, a Jewish person should have a passport.
I still had my father's pocket watch.
He sold it for me.
He gave me back the rest of the money.
With his connections, he was making me a passport.
All I had to do is take a picture.
Had to take a picture without a kerchief on, that me showing my curly hair.
And I have natural curly hair, and only Jewish people have natural curly hair.
I was so frightened, and I had to show my left ear in the picture for some strange reason.
I stayed up all night washing my hair, pulling and making pig tails.
And then a farmer, a Czechoslovenian farmer, a friend of the director, came to the city to see for himself that I'm not-- I don't look too Jewish.
And he was a Protestant too, and he took me to his farm.
I worked very hard there, but they did too.
And that's where I was liberated, in their house, February 2, 1944.
It was more than a year before the war was over.
And when the war was over, when I was liberated rather, I went to the city of Rowne to look for Jewish survivors.
I didn't think my sister was alive anymore.
And the first day I found six people.
You couldn't find a child or an old man.
Whoever survived was more or less my age.
But then I was terribly afraid that the Germans were coming back and forth while they were fighting.
So I find-- found a friend of mine, a girlfriend.
So we went back to Russia to the city of Zhytomir.
It was-- the war was still on.
And over there we worked at a military base as civilians.
And then they sent us to another city, Vinnytsia, that was still-- the Germans were still there.
But as soon as the Russians took over, their military base went in.
In the meantime, my sister was looking for me.
Wherever she went, they told her I left the day before.
And the reason that she knew how to follow me is because she went [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in that-- she was working at the Czech farm.
Where she was working, everybody in the city started to think that she might be Jewish because she was working for terrible people.
No maid ever stayed long enough there.
And this Czech was a relative of the Czech people I was staying with.
So he came to visit one day, and they told them about me.
I was still there.
They told him about me, and he said there was a girl working in the village.
She resembles you a little bit.
I bet that's your sister.
So I gave him a picture.
I gave him a bag of candy I had from the candy factory and a letter.
When he came back, he saw her in the pasture with a cow.
He went over to her, and he asked her, do you have a sister?
She said, no.
He said, it's too bad, because I have a letter for you and a picture.
She said, give it to me.
He said, well, if you don't have a sister, apparently it's not for you.
But he could tell.
So he gave this to her.
And that's how she knew how to follow me.
She went to that Czech farm.
They told that I left a day before.
Then when they told her where I went, she came there, and they told her where I went.
One day here, one day there.
And she came-- she went back to the people she was staying with because they promised her they'll give her shoes, because she didn't have shoes.
And there was a Russian-- when the Russians came in, they told those people, because they were mean people, I'm coming back here tomorrow, this guy said.
If I see that she still doesn't have shoes and she's not sleeping in a bed, I'm gonna throw you out of the house and put you into that bed.
That's how they made shoes for her, because they were afraid, and she started to follow me into Russia.
When she came to the city of Zhytomir, they told her I left an hour ago.
That's when sat down and cried already.
But they said, don't worry.
We have transportation.
We'll take you to the city of Vinnytsia.
She came to that city, and they wouldn't let her in because it was a military base.
Where are your papers?
But a Jewish person didn't have any paper.
We had nothing.
So she said, I don't care.
She's running in.
He said, don't go because I'm going to shoot.
In the meantime, there's a captain standing there who knew me.
He ran in to see if I have a sister.
And she started to run to go inside even though he threatened to-- to shoot.
And next thing I know, she's in my room, and we fell into each other's arm.
But they wouldn't give her a job there, so she and I went back.
It-- yes.
It was the Ukraine, but the Russian Ukraine.
The Soviet Ukraine, right.
Yes.
They were-- they had colonies there.
But they were living in Poland.
Happened to be the Polish Ukraine.
Yes.
Yeah.
And after the war was over, just like we went to Poland, that's how they went to Czechoslovakia.
Yeah.
Yes.
I-- after I had the picture taken and straightened my hair and everything.
He get me the passport.
Like I said, he gave me back the rest of the money.
He didn't keep one cent of it, and gave me the passport.
And I was registered in that village, and my name is Andulka Mazanuk, a Czech name.
Mazanuk.
Not, that was a passport for-- for-- in Germany.
So it's Germany, then.
So I had a German passport.
Not then, no.
After I was liberated and I went to Poland, that's for there you get a Polish passport.
Polish passport was my Jewish name.
I forgot.
No paid-- no one paid attention.
Threw-- we threw 'em away.
Yeah, no.
The picture that was taken for this passport, I have this little picture.
Excuse me?
I still have that picture without a kerchief.
That was-- I was terribly afraid to walk without a kerchief.
I had very blonde and curly hair, but nobody ever could find me without the kerchief.
When I went to bed, I used to keep the kerchief under the pillow.
And once in the middle of the night, when I was on the Czech farm, the Ukrainian partisans knocked at the door, and they came to take horses.
And my farmer was afraid they came for me.
So the first thing I did is put the kerchief on.
And there was my-- I always used the kerchief.
If anybody was around, I'd keep a kerchief on.
To hide my hair.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
No.
The Czechs?
No, the Czech are the most wonderful people.
They were very good people.
And they were-- I worked very hard there, like I said.
But I-- they worked hard too, and they were-- they were very good people.
And they too, the Russians let them go to Czechoslovakia just like they let us go to Poland.
We were Polish citizens.
And also I forgot to mention that one day when I already had a roof over my head with the farmers, I went to look for my father and mother.
I knew where they're supposed to go, and they're supposed to hide.
So one day, dressed as a farm girl, I went to my hometown, a great risk of being recognized.
And I went to these people where my father and mother were supposed to go.
Ostrog, yes.
And when I got there, they told me they never saw my parents.
So I don't know if they didn't let them in or they were caught leaving the ghetto.
But a neighbor said to me, what different does it make?
They're dead.
So by then, there was nothing to do, just run and escape.
And I don't even know where my parents are buried.
Yeah-- no, let's see.
This was 1943.
'43, and actually maybe it was the end of '42.
I was liberated February-- February 2, 1944.
But I backed up to tell about my parents because that was when the ghetto was liquidated--
And that was liquidated in '42.
Yeah.
Well, yeah.
I went for a while there because I was afraid the Germans are gonna come back.
And they did go back and forth a few times.
But then when the war was over, May 9-- May 5, 1945, Polish citizens were allowed to go to Poland.
And the Jewish people left with the idea to go to Palestine.
So we left.
We went to Poland.
And that's from Poland, we made our way to Czechoslovakia, to Austria, and in Austria, we were in DP camps.
I was in D camp-- a DP camp, [?
Velz ?], and then they took us to Bad Gastein in the Alps.
And-- and then later on, we were in the Alps.
That was not bad.
And in Austria, they took us to Ebersburg from Bad Gastein And that's where I met my husband who was-- who was a survivor of seven camps.
Seven?
Seven camps.
And he's the one that there were seven children, and he-- he was 15.
The youngest was six months, and he was the only one that survived.
He was the only survivor and fell in love and got married.
